---
title: "Benchmarking TorchTextClassifiers at INSEE"
author: "Julien Bernardi"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: false
lang: en
execute:
  python: .venv/bin/python
keep-ipynb: false
---

## Introduction

This report presents the work carried out during an internship at **INSEE** (Institut national de la statistique et des études économiques), France's national statistics institute.

The internship focuses on **text classification**, a core natural language processing (NLP) task that consists in assigning a predefined category to a piece of text. At INSEE, this capability is relevant for automatically coding responses to surveys, classifying job descriptions, or categorizing free-text fields in administrative data.

## Motivation

Large language models (LLMs) achieve strong performance on text classification, but they come with significant computational costs. In a production environment processing millions of records, lighter and faster alternatives are desirable.

This internship investigates whether **lightweight neural architectures** — such as FastText-style mean-pooling models and small transformer encoders — can reach competitive accuracy at a fraction of the cost.

## Objectives

The main objectives of this internship are:

1. Benchmark lightweight text classification models across several datasets.
2. Study the impact of key hyperparameters: embedding dimension, number of transformer layers, number of attention heads, vocabulary size, and learning rate.
3. Evaluate the trade-off between model size, training time, and classification accuracy.

## Datasets

The benchmark covers the following publicly available text classification datasets:

| Dataset        | Task                        | Classes |
|----------------|-----------------------------|---------|
| AG News        | News topic classification   | 4       |
| SST-2          | Sentiment analysis          | 2       |
| IMDb           | Sentiment analysis          | 2       |
| Amazon reviews | Sentiment analysis          | 5       |
| 20 Newsgroups  | Topic classification        | 20      |
| SUBJ           | Subjectivity classification | 2       |
| CLINC150       | Intent detection            | 151     |

## Models

Two families of models are compared:

- **FastText-style** (`n_layers=0`): embeddings are averaged across tokens and fed directly to a classification head. No attention, very fast.
- **Transformer encoder** (`n_layers=1,2`): a small transformer block is added on top of the embeddings, with varying numbers of attention heads.

Both families use a **WordPiece tokenizer** trained on the training data, with vocabulary sizes ranging from 2,000 to 20,000 tokens.

## Example Interactive Chart

```{python}
#| echo: false
import plotly.express as px
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "dataset": ["AG News", "SST-2", "IMDb", "Amazon", "20 Newsgroups", "SUBJ", "CLINC150"],
    "accuracy": [0.921, 0.913, 0.895, 0.642, 0.812, 0.934, 0.876],
    "model": ["FastText", "FastText", "Transformer", "Transformer", "FastText", "Transformer", "FastText"],
})

fig = px.bar(
    df, x="dataset", y="accuracy", color="model",
    title="Test Accuracy by Dataset",
    labels={"accuracy": "Test Accuracy", "dataset": "Dataset"},
    range_y=[0.5, 1.0],
)
fig.show()
```

## Experimental Setup

All experiments are tracked with **MLflow** and run on GPU via **Argo Workflows** on the INSEE datalab infrastructure. Each configuration is trained for up to 40 epochs with early stopping (patience = 5).

The key hyperparameters explored are:

- Embedding dimension: 64, 128, 256, 512
- Number of transformer layers: 0, 1, 2
- Number of attention heads: 2, 4, 8
- Vocabulary size: 2,000 / 5,000 / 10,000 / 20,000
- Learning rate: 0.0001, 0.001
